meta.title: G06 Engine Replacement Cost UK — Compare Quotes
meta.description: Every BMW X6 G06 engine — B57, B58, N63, S63, S68 V8. Real UK costs, reliability data and common failures. Get free quotes from 20+ vetted BMW specialists today.

mainEntity.name: How much does a BMW X6 G06 engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned G06 engine typically costs £4,500–£16,000 supply only, depending on engine — the B57 diesel sits at £4,500–£7,500, while the N63 V8 runs £6,000–£11,000 and the S63/S68 in the X6 M can exceed £16,000. Fitting adds £650–£1,500. Use our calculator for a personalised figure.

mainEntity.name: Which BMW X6 G06 engine is the most reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The B58B30C (xDrive40i) — BMW's modular straight-six is widely regarded as one of the most reliable engines BMW has ever produced. It's so good Toyota uses it in the Supra. The B57 diesel (xDrive30d) is a close second — both are proven, durable, and free of the V8's complexity.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the BMW X6 G06?

acceptedAnswer.text: N63 V8 valve stem seal and cooling system failures (M50i), B57 diesel EGR cooler and DPF issues (xDrive30d/40d/M50d), B58 cooling system and coolant loss issues (xDrive40i), and — unrelated to the engine — G06 air suspension system failures (all models).

mainEntity.name: Is the BMW X6 G06 reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: It depends heavily on which engine you choose. The B58 petrol straight-six and B57 diesel straight-six are both reliable and proven. The N63 V8 is a glorious engine but has well-documented hot-vee issues — BMW has revised the N63 for the G06 (TU4 variant), but awareness remains essential. The S63/S68 in the X6 M is spectacular but demands a significant maintenance budget. The air suspension system is a common failure point across all models.

mainEntity.name: Which BMW X6 G06 engine should I avoid?

acceptedAnswer.text: The N63 V8 (M50i) — while improved over earlier N63 variants, it still carries the hot-vee design with valve-stem-seal and cooling system risks. The M50d quad-turbo diesel is also one to avoid unless you're prepared for the complexity and cost of maintaining four turbos. If you want the most trouble-free G06, buy an xDrive30d (B57) or xDrive40i (B58).

mainEntity.name: How do I know if my G06 engine needs replacing?

acceptedAnswer.text: Blue smoke on startup (N63 valve stem seals), coolant loss or overheating (N63, B58), engine warning lights (EGR/DPF issues), or persistent loss of power are the key warning signs. For the air suspension, listen for a noisy compressor or notice the car sitting unevenly. Use our diagnostic calculator for a personalised assessment.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a BMW X6 G06 engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on the car's value. Against a well‑kept £35,000 G06, a £6,500 N63 recon is ~19% of the car's value — safe. Against a tired £18,000 example, the same recon brushes our 70% BMW threshold — walk away. Also factor in the condition of the air suspension system — this can be a significant additional cost.

mainEntity.name: How long do BMW X6 G06 engines last?

acceptedAnswer.text: The B57 diesel can exceed 200,000 miles with proper servicing — regular oil changes (10,000-mile intervals) are essential. The B58 petrol six is similarly durable. The N63 V8's lifespan depends on valve-stem-seal and cooling system maintenance — with proper care, it can reach 150,000+ miles. The S63/S68 in the X6 M is the most fragile — rod bearings are the limiting factor.

mainEntity.name: What is the difference between the G06 and the previous F16 X6?

acceptedAnswer.text: The G06 (2019–present) is the third-generation X6 with a more aggressive design, updated CLAR platform, and BMW's latest B-series engines (B57, B58) alongside revised V8s (N63TU4, S63, S68). The F16 (2014–2019) is the second generation with older N-series engines and a heavier, bulkier design. The G06 is significantly more efficient and refined — see the F16 Generation Page for that model's specifics.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my BMW X6 G06 engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation; block stamping is visible from below on B57/B58/N63/S63/S68 units. Tell us your variant (xDrive30d, xDrive40d, M50d, xDrive40i, M50i, X6 M, X6 M Competition) and year and we'll identify it — 20+ vetted BMW specialists will confirm it for free.

hero.h1: BMW X6 G06 Engines — The Complete UK Guide
hero.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the third-generation X6 G06 — from the B57 six-cylinder diesel to the N63 V8 that powers the M50i, and the S63 V8 in the X6 M Competition. Real UK replacement costs, honest failure data, and the N63 hot-vee issues that still haunt high-mileage V8 cars. Backed by 288+ real UK G06 enquiries in 2025 [BMW-VERIFIED].

engineDatabase.h2: BMW X6 G06 Engine Codes — The Complete Database
engineDatabase.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the third-generation X6 G06 — from the entry-level six-cylinder diesel to the range-topping V8s in the M50i, X6 M and X6 M Competition.

confidenceScore.title: How confident are these ratings?
confidenceScore.text: All reliability ratings are [PROVISIONAL] — no dedicated Engine Page exists yet for any G06 engine family. Ratings are derived from cross-referencing specialist and owner sources. The B58 straight-six is widely considered the most reliable petrol option. The B57 diesel sixes are robust but require diligent oil change intervals. The N63 V8 has well-documented hot-vee issues that BMW partially addressed through extended warranty programs. The S63 in the X6 M shares the N63's architecture but adds more power and stress. The S68 introduced with the LCI adds 48V mild-hybrid technology for improved efficiency.

overview.intro: The G06 is the third generation of the BMW X6, unveiled on 3 July 2019 and positioned as a sportier, coupé-like alternative to the X5. Built on the CLAR platform shared with the G05 X5, the G06 adopts an even more dramatic fastback roofline and aggressive styling. The engine range spans six-cylinder petrol and diesel units through to the range-topping V8s, including the N63 in the M50i and the S63/S68 in the X6 M and X6 M Competition. A 2023 Life Cycle Impulse (LCI) brought the S68 engine with 48V mild-hybrid technology to M Competition models.
overview.keyFacts: Platform: BMW CLAR (shared with G05 X5) • Predecessor: BMW X6 F16 (2014–2019) • Successor: Currently in production (no successor announced) • Sibling body styles in this generation: None (X6 is a standalone SAC)
overview.marketIntelligenceLine: The G06 X6 generated 288 real UK engine enquiries in 2025 [BMW-VERIFIED], concentrated on the B57 diesel and N63 V8 petrol units as these luxury SUVs age out of warranty and maintenance costs become a significant consideration.

items.quote: The B57 is BMW's proven straight-six diesel — the xDrive30d delivers 298 PS and 650 Nm of torque, while the xDrive40d ups that to 352 PS and 700 Nm. Both offer effortless performance and genuine 40+ MPG economy. Unlike the quad-turbo M50d, the twin-turbo B57s are proven, durable, and free of excessive complexity. Regular oil changes at 10,000-mile intervals are the key to long life.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want diesel torque, six-cylinder refinement and proven reliability.

items.quote: The B58 is BMW's modular straight-six and arguably the best engine in the G06 range — 340–381 PS, 450–520 Nm of torque, and a reputation for reliability that rivals Toyota's standards. It's so good Toyota uses it in the Supra. The LCI adds 48V mild-hybrid assistance for smoother stop-start and reduced turbo lag. No V8 complexity, no N63 hot-vee issues.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want the best petrol engine in the range without the V8's running costs.

items.quote: The N63 V8 is a glorious engine — 530 PS, 750 Nm of torque, and a soundtrack that defines the X6 experience. But it carries BMW's 'hot-vee' design, where the turbos sit inside the V of the engine, generating extreme heat. Valve stem seals harden and cause blue smoke on startup; cooling system components fail prematurely. BMW faced a class action over N63 oil consumption issues and introduced the 'N63 Customer Care Package' to address them. The G06 uses a revised N63TU4 variant that is more reliable, but awareness remains essential.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want V8 power and are prepared for V8 maintenance costs.

items.engine: S63B44B / S68B44A (4.4-litre V8, X6 M / X6 M Competition)
items.quote: The S63 is BMW's high-performance V8 — 600–625 PS, 750–800 Nm of torque, making it the most powerful engine in the G06 range. It shares the N63's hot-vee architecture but adds even more power and stress. Rod bearing wear, turbocharger failure, and cooling system issues are well-documented. The S68 introduced with the LCI adds 48V mild-hybrid technology but retains the V8's complexity. A full engine replacement can exceed £16,000.
items.whoItsFor: collectors who want the ultimate G06 X6 and have the budget to maintain it.

items.quote: The xDrive30d delivers 298 PS, 650 Nm of torque, and genuine 40+ MPG economy — the sweet spot in the G06 range. It's the most common diesel, the most proven, and the cheapest to maintain. No quad-turbo complexity, no V8 fuel bills, and an engine that's proven to exceed 200,000 miles with proper servicing.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want the best all-round G06 without the V8's running costs or the M50d's complexity.

items.quote: The xDrive40d is often overlooked in favour of the xDrive30d, but it delivers 352 PS and 700 Nm of torque — a genuine step up in performance. It's the perfect engine for buyers who want effortless overtaking and towing capability without stepping up to the V8's running costs. It's the G06 for buyers who actually drive their cars.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want the ultimate diesel G06 with the best balance of performance and economy.

rows.ownershipVerdict: Best Diesel — proven B57, the one to have

Watch — four turbos, rare, expensive to repair

rows.commonMajorFailure: Cooling system, PCV valve, coolant loss
rows.ownershipVerdict: Best Petrol — reliable, smooth, the sweet spot

rows.commonMajorFailure: Valve stem seals, cooling system, oil consumption
rows.ownershipVerdict: Most Expensive Petrol — V8 complexity, known hot-vee issues
Enthusiast Only — spectacular but extremely costly

economicsRule.title: The G06 rule
economicsRule.text: A £6,000–£11,000 N63 recon against a £20,000–£50,000 G06 sits at 12–55% of the car's value — a repair decision, not a financial one. On a sub-£20,000 high-mileage example, that brushes our 70% BMW threshold — we'll honestly tell you to walk away. Against a well-kept £35,000+ example, the same recon drops to a routine 17–31% — a repair decision, not a financial one. Value the car first, and always check service history on V8 cars. Air suspension is a common additional cost on G06 models.

keyTakeaways.question: Which G06 Costs Least To Own?
keyTakeaways.answer: xDrive30d (B57) — proven six-cylinder diesel, strong economy, no V8 or quad-turbo complexity.

keyTakeaways.question: Which G06 Costs Most To Own?
keyTakeaways.answer: X6 M Competition (S63/S68 V8) — V8 running costs, rod bearing risk, specialist-only maintenance.

keyTakeaways.question: Which G06 Is Best Value?
keyTakeaways.answer: xDrive40i (B58) — the best petrol engine in the range, strong residuals, the one enthusiasts want.

problems.title: N63 V8 Valve Stem Seal & Cooling System Failure
problems.rootCause: The N63's 'hot-vee' design — turbos positioned inside the V of the engine — generates significant heat that accelerates wear on valve stem seals. The seals harden and lose their sealing ability, allowing oil to seep past and into the combustion chamber. The classic symptom is a puff of blue smoke on cold startup. BMW faced a class action over this oil consumption defect and introduced the 'N63 Customer Care Package' to address it. The G06 uses a revised N63TU4 variant that is more reliable, but the underlying design remains a concern.
problems.recommendation: If your N63 V8 smokes on startup, budget for valve-stem-seal replacement immediately — a £1,500–£3,000 repair is dramatically cheaper than the £6,000+ replacement that follows if the catalytic converters are destroyed or the engine overheats. Regular oil changes (5,000-mile intervals) with high-quality oil are essential.

cta.label: Compare N63 engine prices for your G06 M50i →

problems.title: B57 Diesel EGR Cooler & DPF Issues
problems.rootCause: The B57 diesel's EGR cooler can develop leaks or become clogged with soot, leading to coolant loss, poor running, and engine warning lights. The DPF (diesel particulate filter) can clog with repeated short journeys, requiring forced regeneration or replacement. The M50d's quad-turbo setup adds additional complexity and potential failure points.
problems.recommendation: At the first sign of reduced performance or an engine warning light, have the EGR and DPF systems inspected. A £350–£700 service is dramatically cheaper than the £1,200+ replacement that follows a cracked cooler or blocked DPF. Regular long motorway runs help prevent DPF issues.

cta.label: Compare B57 engine prices for your G06 xDrive30d/40d/M50d →

problems.title: B58 Cooling System & Coolant Loss
problems.rootCause: The B58 is widely regarded as BMW's most reliable modern engine, but it's not without issues. The G06 models with the B58 engine have been known to lose coolant quicker than normal. The plastic cooling system components (water pump, thermostat housing, coolant pipes) can become brittle and crack. The PCV (positive crankcase ventilation) valve can also fail, causing whistling noises and rough running.
problems.recommendation: Check coolant levels regularly — if you notice it dropping faster than normal, have the cooling system inspected immediately. A £250–£550 preventative repair is dramatically cheaper than the £3,500+ replacement that follows an overheat. Listen for whistling noises from the engine bay that may indicate PCV valve failure.

cta.label: Compare B58 engine prices for your G06 xDrive40i →

problems.title: G06 Air Suspension System Failure
problems.affectedModels: All G06 variants with rear air suspension (xDrive models, particularly those with optional Dynamic Drive or self-levelling).
problems.rootCause: Not an engine failure, but the most common non-engine complaint on the G06 X6. The air suspension system — air springs, compressor, valve block, and sensors — is prone to failure. The compressor can overheat and fail; air springs can develop leaks; and the system can become unlevel. Replacement costs are significant.
problems.recommendation: Have the air suspension system inspected at every service — a £200–£500 preventative check is dramatically cheaper than the £1,500+ replacement that follows if the compressor fails or the car is left sitting on its bump stops.

cta.label: Compare G06 air suspension repair quotes →

replacementCosts.h2: G06 Engine Replacement Costs — By Engine and Condition

coreVariants.scopeNote: All variants listed are G06 (third-generation X6) only, produced from November 2019 onwards. All models feature xDrive all-wheel drive. The F96 X6 M is the high-performance variant with the S63 V8 (pre-LCI) and S68 V8 (LCI). The M50d features a quad-turbo diesel engine. The LCI (facelift) introduced in 2023 brought the S68 engine to M Competition models.
